title: Incredible storm chaser footage captures tornadoes up close
author: Popkin
summary: The videos in this compilation were shot by storm chasers, people who drive toward the weather everyone else is running from. Some do it for the rush. Others are gathering data on severe storms for research. Either way, they're getting close enough that a bad read on a storm's direction can kill them. —
full_text:
The videos in this compilation were shot by storm chasers, people who drive toward the weather everyone else is running from. Some do it for the rush. Others are gathering data on severe storms for research. Either way, they're getting close enough that a bad read on a storm's direction can kill them.

The footage is hard to stop watching, and hard to watch. The third video below is the one that got me: a tornado so close and so vast it fills the entire frame like a solid grey wall, no sky visible, just storm.

Storm chasing has its own competitive culture, with crews trying to capture the most dramatic footage. In 2013, the El Reno tornado killed three veteran researchers: Tim Samaras, his son Paul, and their chase partner Carl Young. The storm shifted direction and exploded in size without warning. It stretched 2.6 miles across, the widest tornado ever recorded.
